text: What is the correct sequence of events a process must follow when utilizing a system resource?
info: In the standard system model, a process must first request a resource, use it once the request is granted, and finally release it so other processes can access it.

answers.text: Release, request, use
Use, request, release
Request, release, use
Request, use, release

text: In a Resource-Allocation Graph, how is an 'assignment edge' distinguished from a 'request edge'?
info: In the Resource-Allocation Graph, Pi → Rj represents a request edge (process Pi requesting Rj), while Rj → Pi represents an assignment edge (Rj being held by Pi).

answers.text: An assignment edge is a dashed line whereas a request edge is a solid line pointing from the process to the resource.
A request edge represents a process holding a resource, and an assignment edge represents a process waiting for a resource.
An assignment edge is a directed edge from a resource to a process, while a request edge points from a process to a resource.
An assignment edge is only used for multiple instances of a resource, while request edges are used for single instances of a resource.

text: Which of the following describes the 'No preemption' condition required for a deadlock to occur?
info: No preemption means resources cannot be forcefully taken from a process; they must be released voluntarily by the process itself.

answers.text: A process holding a resource is prevented from requesting any additional resources until it finishes its current task.
Processes cannot be interrupted by the operating system scheduler while they are in the 'Running' state.
A resource can only be released voluntarily by the process holding it after its task is complete.
Only one process at a time can use a resource, and no other process can interrupt that usage.

text: If a Resource-Allocation Graph contains a cycle, which statement regarding deadlocks is true?
info: If each resource type has exactly one instance, a cycle in the graph is synonymous with a deadlock. If there are multiple instances, a cycle only indicates the possibility of a deadlock.

answers.text: A deadlock has definitely occurred, regardless of the number of instances for each resource type.
The presence of a cycle is a necessary and sufficient condition for deadlock in systems with multiple instances per resource.
If there is only one instance per resource type, then a cycle implies that a deadlock has occurred.
A deadlock can only exist if there are multiple instances per resource type; single instances cannot deadlock.

text: Which condition is met when a set of processes $\{P_0, P_1, \dots, P_n\}$ exists such that $P_0$ waits for $P_1$, $P_1$ waits for $P_2$, and $P_n$ waits for $P_0$?
info: Circular wait is the condition where a closed chain of processes exists, each waiting for a resource held by the next process in the chain.

answers.text: Mutual exclusion
No preemption
Hold and wait
Circular wait

text: How is the 'Hold and wait' condition characterized in deadlock theory?
info: Hold and wait occurs when a process does not release its current resources while it is blocked waiting for new ones.

answers.text: A process holding at least one resource is waiting to acquire additional resources held by other processes.
The system holds all resources in a central pool while processes wait for a global signal to begin execution.
A process must wait for a specific time quantum to expire before it is allowed to hold any non-sharable resources.
A process is suspended in the wait queue because it does not have the priority to hold any system resources.

text: What are the two types of nodes (vertices) present in a Resource-Allocation Graph?
info: The set of vertices V is partitioned into P (all processes) and R (all resource types).

answers.text: Source nodes and Sink nodes
Request nodes and Assignment nodes
Active nodes and Waiting nodes
Process nodes and Resource nodes

text: In a system with multiple instances of resource types, why might a cycle in the Resource-Allocation Graph NOT indicate a deadlock?
info: With multiple instances, a cycle suggests a potential deadlock, but if a process outside the cycle releases an instance of a resource type involved in the cycle, the deadlock may be avoided.

answers.text: Cycles are only valid indicators of deadlock when the graph is converted into a Wait-for graph containing only resource nodes.
The operating system may automatically preempt resources from processes involved in a cycle every few milliseconds.
Resource instances not involved in the cycle could be released by other processes, breaking the stall.
The cycle might involve processes that are currently in the 'Ready' state rather than the 'Waiting' state.

text: Which of the following is considered a resource type in the system model?
info: Typical resource types include CPU cycles, memory space, and I/O devices.

answers.text: Stack pointers
Program counters
CPU cycles
Process IDs

text: Deadlock can only arise if how many conditions hold simultaneously?
info: All four conditions (Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, and Circular Wait) must hold at the same time for a deadlock to occur.

answers.text: Two
One
Three
Four

text: Which statement best describes the 'Mutual exclusion' condition?
info: Mutual exclusion ensures that a resource instance cannot be shared by multiple processes simultaneously.

answers.text: Resources are excluded from the system allocation if they are being used by multiple processes.
Processes are excluded from the CPU if they do not hold at least one resource.
All processes in the system must be excluded from memory during a context switch.
Only one process at a time can use a specific instance of a resource.

text: What is represented by a process node and a resource node with four internal dots in a Resource-Allocation Graph?
info: In the graph, dots within a resource rectangle represent the number of instances ($W_i$) of that resource type.

answers.text: A process requesting four different resource types simultaneously.
A resource type that has four total instances available in the system.
A cycle involving four processes and four resources.
A resource type that requires four processes to access it at the same time.

text: Which of the following is an approach where the system allows a deadlock to occur and then attempts to fix it?
info: This approach involves periodically checking for deadlocks and using a recovery scheme once one is detected.

answers.text: Deadlock avoidance
The Ostrich algorithm
Deadlock prevention
Deadlock detection and recovery

text: What is the primary difference between deadlock prevention and deadlock avoidance?
info: Prevention focuses on ensuring one of the four necessary conditions cannot hold. Avoidance requires the system to know the maximum resource needs of processes beforehand to ensure the system never enters an unsafe state.

answers.text: Avoidance is a proactive strategy to break one of the four necessary conditions, while prevention is reactive.
Prevention limits how requests are made, while avoidance uses additional a priori information to stay in a safe state.
Prevention is used for single-instance resources, while avoidance is only for multi-instance resources.
Prevention uses algorithms like Banker's, while avoidance uses Wait-for graphs.

text: Which strategy for handling deadlocks is actually used by most modern operating systems, including UNIX?
info: Many operating systems ignore the deadlock problem due to the high cost of prevention and avoidance compared to the relative infrequency of deadlocks in standard usage.

answers.text: Strict deadlock prevention by resource ordering.
Dynamic deadlock avoidance using the Banker's algorithm.
Continuous deadlock detection with automatic process rollbacks.
Ignoring the problem and pretending deadlocks never occur.

text: What is a major downside of using deadlock prevention or avoidance strategies?
info: Strict requirements in prevention and avoidance can lead to resources staying idle or processes waiting indefinitely (starvation).

answers.text: Low resource utilization and potential for process starvation.
Increased risk of hardware failure due to complex algorithms.
Significant increase in the number of context switches.
The system becomes unable to handle multi-threaded processes.

text: Deadlock avoidance requires that each process declares what information before execution?
info: Avoidance models require a priori information, specifically the maximum demand a process will place on each resource type.

answers.text: The maximum number of resources of each type it may ever need.
The exact time at which each resource will be requested.
The sequence of system calls it will perform during its lifecycle.
The priority level relative to all other concurrent processes.

text: Which approach is most suitable when the overhead of checking for deadlocks outweighs the cost of the deadlock itself?
info: Ignoring deadlocks (the Ostrich algorithm) is common when the probability of deadlock is low and the overhead of handling it is high.

answers.text: Deadlock detection
Resource preemption
Deadlock avoidance
Ignoring the deadlock

text: What does a 'safe state' guarantee in the context of deadlock avoidance?
info: A safe state ensures a safe sequence exists where all processes can eventually complete their execution by acquiring their maximum requested resources.

answers.text: That the system will eventually terminate all deadlocked processes.
That no process will ever have to wait for a resource.
That there exists a sequence of processes such that all can satisfy their maximum needs.
That the Resource-Allocation Graph will never contain a cycle.

text: Which statement best describes the relationship between an unsafe state and a deadlock?
info: An unsafe state means there is a *possibility* of deadlock, but it does not mean one has already occurred. Deadlock is a subset of the unsafe state space.

answers.text: Every deadlock is an unsafe state, but not every unsafe state is a deadlock.
An unsafe state is the same thing as a deadlock.
A system in an unsafe state is guaranteed to deadlock within a finite number of steps.
A system is only in an unsafe state if a deadlock has already been detected by the safety algorithm.

text: How does dynamic deadlock avoidance appraisal help maintain system viability?
info: Avoidance algorithms dynamically check if granting a request leads to an unsafe state, thereby preventing circular wait.

answers.text: By always granting resource requests immediately to keep the CPU utilization at 100%.
By forcing all processes to release their resources every time a new process enters the system.
By examining the resource-allocation state to ensure a circular-wait condition can never occur.
By killing any process that requests more than the currently available resources.

text: What is a primary trade-off when moving from ignoring deadlocks to implementing strict prevention?
info: Prevention and avoidance strategies often lead to poorer resource utilization because they are conservative.

answers.text: Resource utilization typically drops as the system imposes more constraints on allocation.
System security decreases as prevention requires more administrative privileges.
The complexity of the hardware must increase to support the prevention logic.
The number of processes the system can handle simultaneously increases significantly.

text: When considering deadlock handling, which of the following is characterized by 'permitting risky allocations but relying on real-time checks'?
info: Deadlock detection allows allocations to proceed without checking for safety, then later identifies if those allocations led to a deadlock.

answers.text: Deadlock Avoidance
Deadlock Detection
The Bankers' Algorithm
Deadlock Prevention

text: If a system is designed to absolutely prohibit cyclical waiting by structural rules, which strategy is being employed?
info: Prevention works by restraining the way requests can be made to ensure one of the conditions (like circular wait) can never occur.

answers.text: Prevention
Detection
Avoidance
Recovery

text: Which deadlock prevention method is specifically designed to eliminate the 'Circular Wait' condition?
info: Imposing a strict order on resource types prevents the formation of cycles because a process holding a high-numbered resource cannot request a low-numbered one.

answers.text: Requiring processes to request and be allocated all resources before execution begins.
Ensuring that all resources are sharable, such as read-only files.
Imposing a total ordering of all resource types and requiring processes to request them in increasing order.
Preempting resources from processes that are currently in the waiting state.

text: Under the 'Hold and Wait' prevention protocol, what must be true when a process requests a resource?
info: A common protocol to break Hold and Wait is to allow a process to request resources only when it has none allocated to it.

answers.text: The process must not hold any other resources currently.
The process must have completed at least 50% of its total computation time.
The process must already hold at least one resource of a higher priority.
The resource must have multiple instances available in the system.

text: What is a major disadvantage of the 'all-or-nothing' resource allocation strategy used for deadlock prevention?
info: Processes requiring multiple resources that are often busy may wait indefinitely, leading to starvation.

answers.text: It increases the likelihood of a circular wait condition.
It requires the system to maintain complex Wait-for graphs for all processes.
Starvation is possible for processes that need many popular resources.
It results in high resource utilization because resources are held for longer periods.

text: How can the 'No Preemption' condition be broken as a prevention strategy?
info: If a process requests a resource that is unavailable, preemption can be forced by making the process release its current holdings to be added to the pool of available resources.

answers.text: By ensuring that every process has a static maximum claim that cannot be exceeded.
By allowing the OS to kill any process that has been waiting for more than 10 seconds.
By forcing a process to release all its currently held resources if its new request cannot be immediately satisfied.
By assigning a unique numerical ID to every resource instance and sorting them.

text: Why is 'Mutual Exclusion' generally NOT a feasible condition to break for deadlock prevention?
info: Certain resources cannot be shared without causing data corruption or functional failure; therefore, mutual exclusion must hold for them.

answers.text: Some resources, like printers or mutexes, are inherently non-sharable and must be accessed by only one process.
Breaking mutual exclusion would cause the Banker's algorithm to fail during the request phase.
Because most physical resources are already sharable by design.
Sharable resources, like read-only files, are the primary cause of deadlocks in multi-user systems.

text: If a system uses total resource ordering, and a process holds resource $R_5$, which of the following requests would be denied to prevent a circular wait?
info: In an increasing order enumeration, a process must request resources in order. If it holds $R_5$, it can only request resources $R_i$ where $i > 5$.

answers.text: $R_5$ (requesting a second instance)

text: In the context of 'No Preemption' prevention, what happens to the resources that were held by a process that is now waiting?
info: Preempted resources are added back to the pool, and the process is put into a state where it waits to regain both its old and newly requested resources.

answers.text: They are immediately deleted to avoid data leaks to other processes.
They are added to the list of resources for which the process is waiting.
They are converted into sharable resources for the duration of the wait.
They are locked until the process can regain its execution state.

text: Which of these is a valid protocol to satisfy the 'Hold and Wait' prevention requirement?
info: Allowing a process to request resources ONLY when it has none allocated is a common method to break Hold and Wait.

answers.text: Allow a process to request a resource only if it already holds another instance of that same type.
Require a process to release all its resources before requesting new ones.
Only grant resources if a safe sequence can be determined for the whole system.
Manually restart any process that has been in the 'Hold' state for more than one CPU cycle.

text: Total ordering of all resource types primarily prevents which deadlock condition?
info: By requiring resource acquisition in a specific sequence, the possibility of a closed loop (a cycle) in resource requests is removed.

answers.text: Hold and Wait
Mutual Exclusion
No Preemption
Circular Wait

text: What is the specific penalty of forcing 'Hold and Wait' prevention via all-or-nothing allocation?
info: If a process requests all resources at the start, it will hold resources it might not need until the end of its execution, preventing others from using them.

answers.text: Resources may be allocated but unused for long periods, leading to low utilization.
Cycle formation becomes much more likely as processes compete for large blocks of resources.
Reduced throughput due to the complexity of the Resource-Allocation Graph.
Context switching time increases because the OS must save the state of all allocated resources.

text: In a prevention strategy breaking 'No Preemption', when will a waiting process be restarted?
info: The process must wait until all resources—both the ones it was forced to release and the new ones it needs—are available.

answers.text: After a fixed rollback interval calculated from its total CPU time.
If it is the highest priority process in the system, regardless of resource availability.
As soon as any one of its requested resources becomes available.
Only when it can regain its old resources as well as the new ones it is requesting.

text: By definition, a 'safe state' is one where:
info: A state is safe if there exists a 'safe sequence' of all processes.

answers.text: All processes have had their resource requests satisfied immediately.
The system is currently free of any cycles in the Resource-Allocation Graph.
The number of available resources exceeds the number of processes in the system.
There exists at least one order in which all processes can finish their work without deadlocking.

text: In the Banker's Algorithm, if you are given the `Max` and `Allocation` matrices, how do you calculate the `Need` matrix?
info: The Need matrix represents how many more resources each process can still request, which is its maximum claim minus what it currently holds.

answers.text: $Need[i,j] = Max[i,j] + Allocation[i,j]$
$Need[i,j] = Max[i,j] - Allocation[i,j]$
$Need[i,j] = Available[j] - Max[i,j]$
$Need[i,j] = Allocation[i,j] - Max[i,j]$

text: What does a 'claim edge' ($P_i \to R_j$) represent in an extended Resource-Allocation Graph?
info: Claim edges are represented by dashed lines and indicate potential future requests for resource types.

answers.text: Process $P_i$ may request resource $R_j$ at some time in the future.
Process $P_i$ is requesting its final resource before completion.
Process $P_i$ is currently being blocked by resource $R_j$.
Resource $R_j$ has been permanently assigned to process $P_i$.

text: When can a resource request in a single-instance system be granted safely according to the Resource-Allocation Graph algorithm?
info: In avoidance for single-instance resources, we must ensure that adding an assignment edge doesn't create a cycle, even including potential future claim edges.

answers.text: Only if converting the request edge to an assignment edge does not result in a cycle.
Only if all other processes are currently in the 'Safe' region of the graph.
If the process making the request has the highest priority value.
Whenever there is at least one dot visible in the resource node.

text: What is the primary role of the `Available` vector in the Banker's Algorithm?
info: Available[j] = k means there are currently k instances of resource type Rj available.

answers.text: To list which processes have finished their execution.
To track the total number of resource instances in the hardware.
To store the maximum number of resources any single process will ever need.
To track how many instances of each resource type are currently free to be allocated.

text: In the safety algorithm, why is the `Finish` vector initialized to `false` for all processes?
info: The safety algorithm simulates the execution of processes. `Finish[i] = true` means the algorithm has identified a way for process i to finish using the current resources.

answers.text: To ensure that deadlocked processes are not mistakenly identified as being in a safe state.
Because no processes are allowed to finish until the safety algorithm completes.
To indicate that all processes still require at least one instance of every resource type.
To allow the system to simulate process termination during its safety check.

text: According to the Resource-Request Algorithm, what is the first check the system performs when process $P_i$ requests resources?
info: The system first checks if the request exceeds the process's maximum claim (declared need). If it does, an error is raised.

answers.text: Is $Request_i \le Need_i$?
Is $Request_i \le Available$?
Is $Allocation_i + Request_i \le Max_i$?
Is $Finish[i] == false$?

text: What happens in the Banker's Algorithm if a simulated allocation leads to an unsafe state?
info: Avoidance requires that the system only commit to an allocation if the resulting state is safe. If unsafe, the state is rolled back and the process waits.

answers.text: The process is immediately terminated.
The request is denied, and the process must wait while the old resource-allocation state is restored.
The system ignores the safety check and grants the request to improve utilization.
The system enters a recovery phase to preempt resources from other processes.

text: In the safety algorithm, what is the formula for updating the `Work` vector when a process can be satisfied?
info: If a process $P_i$ can finish, the resources it currently holds ($Allocation_i$) are returned to the pool of available resources ($Work$).

answers.text: $Work = Work - Need_i$
$Work = Work + Available$
$Work = Max_i - Allocation_i$
$Work = Work + Allocation_i$

text: When a process finishes its task in the Banker's algorithm, what must it do with its resources?
info: A requirement of the Banker's algorithm is that when a process gets all its resources, it must eventually return them.

answers.text: Permanently lock them to prevent other processes from entering an unsafe state.
Return them to the system in a finite amount of time.
Transfer them directly to the next process in the safe sequence.
Hold them until all processes in the system have also finished.

text: Consider a system with 12 Instances of R1. P0 has 5, P1 has 2, P2 has 2. (Available = 3). Max needs are P0: 10, P1: 4, P2: 9. Is this state safe?
info: P1 needs 2 more (4-2), which can be satisfied by Available (3). When P1 finishes, it releases 2, making Available 5. P0 needs 5 more (10-5), which can then be satisfied. After P0 finishes, P2's needs can be met.

answers.text: Yes, sequence <P1, P2, P0> is safe.
Yes, sequence <P1, P0, P2> is safe.
No, there is no way to satisfy P0 or P2 after P1 finishes.
No, because the total Max needs (23) exceed total resources (12).

text: Which matrix in the Banker's Algorithm represents the resources currently held by each process?
info: Wait-for graphs are used in systems where each resource type has only one instance.

answers.text: Max
Allocation
Need
Available

text: What is the core structural difference between a Resource-Allocation Graph and its corresponding Wait-for graph?
info: A wait-for graph is a simplified version of a RAG used for single-instance resource systems where an edge $P_i \to P_j$ exists if $P_i$ is waiting for a resource held by $P_j$.

answers.text: Wait-for graphs use dashed lines to represent current allocations, whereas RAGs use solid lines.
The Wait-for graph includes 'claim edges' while the Resource-Allocation Graph does not.
The Resource-Allocation Graph contains only processes, whereas Wait-for graphs include resources.
The Wait-for graph removes resource nodes, leaving only edges between processes.

text: In the deadlock detection algorithm for multiple instances, if a process $P_i$ has zero resources currently allocated, how is its `Finish[i]` value initialized?
info: If a process has no resources allocated ($Allocation_i = 0$), it cannot be part of a deadlock cycle. Thus, `Finish[i]` is initialized to true.

answers.text: It depends on its current request vector.
true
false
null

text: How is a deadlock officially identified using the detection algorithm's results?
info: If `Finish[i]` remains false, it means the system could not find a sequence where process $P_i$ could obtain its requested resources and finish.

answers.text: If the `Work` vector equals the `Available` vector at the end of the simulation.
If any process has its `Request_i > Available` condition met.
If any process $P_i$ still has `Finish[i] == false` after the algorithm terminates.
If a cycle is detected in the `Max` needs matrix.

text: Which factor is typically considered when selecting a 'victim' process for recovery via preemption?
info: Victim selection focuses on minimizing cost, often looking at how long a process has computed and how many resources it uses.

answers.text: The number of times the process has been preempted in the past year.
The PID (Process ID) of the process.
The amount of resources the process has consumed so far.
The time of day the process was started.

text: What is the risk of selecting a victim for resource preemption based solely on 'minimum cost' without other factors?
info: To prevent starvation, the number of rollbacks must be included in the cost factor when choosing which process to preempt.

answers.text: Starvation, where the same process is always picked as the victim.
Throughput will drop to zero.
The system will enter a safe state too quickly, wasting CPU time.
The detection algorithm will take $O(n^3)$ operations instead of $O(n^2)$.

text: When recovering from a deadlock, what does 'Rollback' entail?
info: A rollback involves returning the system to a known good state (checkpoints) and restarting work from there.

answers.text: Moving a process from the 'Waiting' queue to the 'Ready' queue immediately.
Returning a process to a safe state and restarting it from that state.
Increasing the memory allocation for all deadlocked processes.
Restarting the entire operating system to clear all locks.

text: What is the time complexity (order of operations) of the detection algorithm for a system with $n$ processes and $m$ resource types?
info: Detecting deadlock with multiple resource instances requires a complexity of $O(m \times n^2)$.

answers.text: $O(m \times n^2)$
$O(n \times m^2)$

text: In the process termination strategy for recovery, what is the 'most extreme' but simplest method?
info: While extreme, aborting all processes involved in the deadlock is a fast way to clear the condition, though it discards potentially large amounts of work.

answers.text: Suspend the lowest priority processes in the system.
Abort all deadlocked processes simultaneously.
Preempt all non-sharable resources from all processes.
Abort one process at a time until the cycle is broken.

text: What determines how often the deadlock detection algorithm should be invoked?
info: Invocation frequency depends on how often deadlocks occur; if they happen often, the algorithm should be run frequently to minimize work loss.

answers.text: The specific ordering of resources used in the prevention strategy.
The number of claim edges currently in the Resource-Allocation Graph.
The speed of the CPU and the amount of available RAM.
The frequency with which deadlocks are likely to occur and the number of processes affected.

text: In the context of deadlock recovery, which factor might make an operating system choose to abort a 'batch' process over an 'interactive' one?
info: The type of process (interactive vs batch) is a cost factor; user-facing processes are usually preserved if possible.

answers.text: Batch processes typically require fewer resource instances.
Interactive processes usually have higher PIDs.
Batch processes are impossible to roll back to a safe state.
Aborting an interactive process disproportionately affects the user experience.

text: When using a Wait-for graph for detection, what indicates that a deadlock exists?
info: Similar to the RAG for single-instance resources, a cycle in the wait-for graph indicates a deadlock.

answers.text: The presence of a cycle in the graph.
The absence of any request edges.
A node with no outgoing edges.
A process node connected to multiple resource nodes.

text: Why is it potentially problematic to invoke the detection algorithm only when a resource request cannot be granted immediately?
info: If the detection is run too infrequently, multiple deadlocks can pile up, making it hard to identify the root cause or the minimum number of rollbacks needed.

answers.text: The algorithm might only find one cycle even if multiple cycles exist, failing to clear the whole deadlock.
The complexity would increase from $O(m \times n^2)$ to $O(n!)$ because requests are sequential.
It would identify too many processes as being deadlocked.
We might not be able to tell which of the many deadlocked processes 'caused' the deadlock if many cycles have formed.
